source: Invest Like the Best — Tech and consumer growth investing (EP.149)
summary: Artificial intelligence is in the bottom of the first inning and will be the next major platform wave, enabled by mobile-generated data.
summary_extended: Baker argues that artificial intelligence represents a foundational platform wave still in its earliest stages ('bottom of the first inning'), comparable in magnitude to prior platform shifts like mobile and the internet. He contends that AI progress has been driven almost entirely by two factors — exponentially increasing compute (cloud infrastructure) and the explosion of training data generated by mobile phones — rather than by algorithmic innovation. Critically, he cites Google and Microsoft Research papers establishing that for every order-of-magnitude increase in training data, AI model quality doubles, creating a compounding feedback loop that structurally advantages companies with the largest proprietary data assets. The investment implication is that large-scale data holders — particularly those with massive consumer-facing platforms generating continuous behavioral data — are positioned to widen their AI moats over time, making them long-term structural winners in the AI era.
detail: AI advances have been driven almost entirely by increased compute (cloud) and dramatically more data (from mobile phones), not algorithmic breakthroughs. For every order of magnitude increase in training data, AI quality doubles (per Google and Microsoft Research papers). This creates powerful feedback loops for large-scale data holders.
confidence_evidence: Baker presents this as a foundational structural view with specific citations to research papers and quantified relationships.
quote_fragment: we are at the bottom of the first inning in the AI revolution

ticker_audit.reasoning: Google is explicitly cited as a source of the research Baker references and is the canonical example of a large-scale data holder with massive proprietary consumer data and cloud compute, making it a direct beneficiary of the feedback loop Baker describes.
